translation: Certainly has Allah heard the speech of the one who argues with you, [O Muhammad], concerning her husband and directs her complaint to Allah. And Allah hears your dialogue; indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing
commentary:
قَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّتِى تُجَـٰدِلُكَ فِى زَوْجِهَا وَتَشْتَكِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَآ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ

**Allah has certainly heard the speech of the one who argues with you concerning her husband and complains to Allah. And Allah hears your dialogue. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing.**

This ayah was revealed in response to a particular domestic crisis that occurred among the companions. A woman named Khawlah bint Tha'labah (RA) came to the Prophet ﷺ in desperate circumstances. Her husband had pronounced dhihar upon her—an ugly pre-Islamic practice where a man would say to his wife, "You are to me like the back of my mother" (anti 'alayya ka dhahri ummi). This statement was meant to sever the marriage relationship permanently while leaving the woman in limbo—neither married nor divorced, unable to remarry, yet cut off from her husband.

Khawlah (RA) had small children and found herself in an impossible situation. She approached the Prophet ﷺ seeking guidance, but he remained silent. This silence itself was a form of revelation. The Prophet ﷺ sometimes responded immediately to questions, while at other times something in his heart would tell him that revelation was coming about this specific matter, and he should wait. His silence indicated that Allah ﷻ was about to address this injustice directly through the Quran.

As Khawlah (RA) continued pleading her case, desperate and weeping about her children and her situation, she turned to Allah ﷻ in supplication. At that moment, this ayah was revealed: قَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّتِى تُجَـٰدِلُكَ (Allah has certainly heard the speech of the one who argues with you).

The word تُجَـٰدِلُكَ refers to her debating or arguing with the Prophet ﷺ about her husband. The term زَوْج  in Arabic can refer to either a husband or wife—it encompasses the meaning of spouse. The word وَتَشْتَكِىٓ  means she was complaining intensely to Allah ﷻ. This is the mufa'alah form, which indicates repeated, prolonged complaint. She wasn't just mentioning her problem once; she was persistently, desperately appealing to Allah ﷻ for help.

What makes this ayah profound is that Allah ﷻ says He "already heard" her complaint—قَدْ سَمِعَ. The particle قَدْ indicates completion and emphasis. Before the ayah was even revealed, before anyone else validated her pain, Allah ﷻ had already heard her. This teaches us that no complaint made to Allah ﷻ goes unheard, no matter how powerless we feel.

The ayah continues: وَٱللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَآ (And Allah hears your dialogue). The word تَحَاوُر comes from the root ح-و-ر and refers to a mutual exchange, a back-and-forth conversation. It specifically suggests a courteous dialogue. The choice of this word indicates that despite her desperation, the exchange between Khawlah (RA) and the Prophet ﷺ remained respectful. Had it been a more intense or contentious conversation, the Quran would have used the more forceful term حِوَار .

The ayah concludes with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing). These two divine attributes appear together frequently in the Quran. He doesn't just hear our words—He sees our circumstances, our tears, our helplessness. The combination assures us that Allah ﷻ is fully aware of both what we express and what we cannot express.

Several powerful lessons emerge from this opening ayah. First, Allah ﷻ took a domestic dispute—a private matter between husband and wife—and made it a subject of Quranic revelation. This demonstrates that no injustice is too small for Allah's attention, even when it occurs behind closed doors. Second, the entire surah is named Al-Mujadila (The Pleading Woman) after this woman's complaint, immortalizing her struggle in the Quran until the Day of Judgment. Third, not one but four ayahs address this single issue, showing how seriously Islam takes the wellbeing of women and the integrity of family relationships. Fourth, her complaint is described as legitimate and important enough that Allah ﷻ responded directly through His revelation.

translation: Those who pronounce thihar among you [to separate] from their wives - they are not [consequently] their mothers. Their mothers are none but those who gave birth to them. And indeed, they are saying an objectionable statement and a falsehood. But indeed, Allah is Pardoning and Forgiving
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِهِم مَّا هُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِهِمْ ۖ إِنْ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى وَلَدْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنكَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ

**Those who pronounce dhihar among you regarding their wives—they are not their mothers. Their mothers are none but those who gave birth to them. And indeed, they are saying an objectionable statement and a falsehood. But indeed, Allah is Pardoning and Forgiving.**

This ayah directly addresses and abolishes the jahiliyyah practice of dhihar. Allah ﷻ declares with absolute clarity: مَّا هُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِهِمْ (they are not their mothers). The مَا here is for emphatic negation—absolutely not, under no circumstances. No matter what words a husband utters in anger, his wife remains his wife. Calling her his mother doesn't change her actual relationship to him.

The ayah continues with even stronger emphasis: إِنْ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى وَلَدْنَهُمْ (Their mothers are none but those who gave birth to them). Here Allah ﷻ uses the feminine form اللائي meaning "those women who." The restriction is absolute—your mother is exclusively the woman who gave birth to you. Words don't change biological and legal realities.

This principle extends across all Islamic relationships. The bonds of kinship and marriage are مِيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا —a solemn covenant, as the Quran describes marriage. These relationships don't dissolve through exaggerated speech. You cannot severe them through emotional outbursts or angry declarations.

This applies in reverse as well. Out of love, you might call someone your son, but that doesn't make him your biological son. The Quran addresses this in Surah Al-Ahzab regarding Zayd ibn Harithah (RA), whom the Prophet ﷺ had adopted. Allah ﷻ revealed that calling someone your son doesn't change his lineage, his inheritance rights, or his identity. Similarly, we use terms like "uncle" or "auntie" out of respect, but these are social courtesies—they don't create actual familial bonds with legal implications.

Even within extended families, cousins who grow up together like siblings may feel comfortable around each other, but Islamic boundaries still apply. Saying "she's just like my sister" doesn't make her your mahram. The statement doesn't change the reality of the relationship or the rules that govern it.

The ugliest application of this misuse of words, however, is within marriage—when anger leads to pronouncements meant to destroy the relationship. Allah ﷻ characterizes such speech in the strongest terms: وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنكَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا (And indeed, they are saying an objectionable statement and a falsehood).

The word مُنكَر  comes from the root ن-ك-ر, which means something unknown, unrecognizable, or denied. In Islamic terminology, munkar refers to evil, but its linguistic root reveals something deeper. It describes something so alien to decent people that they wouldn't even recognize it. In a healthy society with proper values, such speech would be unknown—no one would have heard such vile words. The fact that it's "unknown" to good people indicates it must be evil. When you raise children in a sheltered, wholesome environment where they never hear foul language, backbiting, or cursing, the first time they encounter such words, they don't even understand them. That speech is munkar to them—completely foreign.

The phrase مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ  is significant. It means "of the things that can be said"—implying there were other ways to express frustration, other words available. But these men chose the worst possible statement. They didn't think it through; they reached for the most hurtful, destructive words they could find.

Then Allah ﷻ adds وَزُورًا —a falsehood. But زُور is not just any lie. It specifically means a false accusation intended to hurt someone, or false testimony meant to harm someone in legal proceedings. It's a serious term that appears elsewhere in the Quran: وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَشْهَدُونَ ٱلزُّورَ (And those who do not testify falsely). This is slander with malicious intent.

After describing this practice in such harsh terms, the ayah concludes unexpectedly: وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (But indeed, Allah is Pardoning and Forgiving). Both عَفُوّ  and غَفُور  relate to forgiveness, but with distinct meanings. 'Afuww suggests pardoning to the extent that the sin is wiped away, while Ghafoor indicates covering and concealing the sin. The message is clear: Yes, this is a serious transgression, but seek Allah's forgiveness, don't let it happen again, and move forward. The marriage remains intact, but the husband must make amends.

translation: And those who pronounce thihar from their wives and then [wish to] go back on what they said - then [there must be] the freeing of a slave before they touch one another. That is what you are admonished thereby; and Allah is Acquainted with what you do
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا۟ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

**And those who pronounce dhihar from their wives and then retract what they said—then the freeing of a slave before they touch one another. That is what you are instructed. And Allah is Aware of what you do.**

This ayah establishes the expiation (kaffara) for dhihar. The phrase ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا۟ (then they return from what they said) means they take back their statement—they apologize, express regret, want to undo what they said in anger. "I'm sorry, I didn't mean it, I take it back."

The penalty is clear: فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ (the freeing of a slave). The word تَحْرِير literally means liberation, and رَقَبَة means neck—a reference to the slave whose neck is freed from bondage. This must occur مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا (before they touch one another). The verb يَتَمَآسَّا comes from the root م-س-س meaning to touch, but it's used here as a euphemism for physical intimacy between husband and wife. Until the husband pays this penalty, the couple cannot resume their intimate relationship.

Why such a serious consequence? Because words matter, especially words spoken within the sacred bond of marriage. This is not just a fine you pay and forget about. Freeing a slave required significant financial resources and conscious intention. It forced the husband to confront the gravity of what he said and to make meaningful amends.

The ayah continues: ذَٰلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ (That is what you are instructed). The word تُوعَظُونَ comes from وَعْظ , which means counsel or admonishment. This isn't just a command—it's guidance given for your own benefit, advice meant to reform your character.

Finally: وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (And Allah is Aware of what you do). The word خَبِير  means fully informed, possessing complete knowledge of something. Allah ﷻ knows what transpires in the privacy of your home. He knows whether you paid the expiation, whether you're sincere in your repentance, whether you've truly reformed. This serves as both a warning and a comfort—a warning that you cannot hide your actions, and a comfort that He understands your struggles.

translation: And he who does not find [a slave] - then a fast for two months consecutively before they touch one another; and he who is unable - then the feeding of sixty poor persons. That is for you to believe [completely] in Allah and His Messenger; and those are the limits [set by] Allah. And for the disbelievers is a painful punishment
commentary:
فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعَامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِينًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

**And he who does not find [a slave]—then a fast for two months consecutively before they touch one another. And he who is unable—then the feeding of sixty poor persons. That is for you to believe in Allah and His Messenger. And those are the limits of Allah. And for the disbelievers is a painful punishment.**

This ayah provides alternatives for those who cannot free a slave. The phrase فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ (and whoever does not find) doesn't just mean he looked around and couldn't locate a slave to purchase and free. It encompasses not having the financial means—being incapable of affording this expiation.

The alternative is فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ (fasting for two months consecutively). The word مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ  is crucial—it means consecutive, without break. You cannot fast a few days, take a break, and resume later. It must be continuous: sixty consecutive days, and this must occur مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا (before they touch one another)—before resuming marital intimacy.

But what if even this is impossible? Perhaps the person is elderly, or chronically ill, or physically unable to fast for such an extended period. Imagine an old man who got angry at his wife and pronounced dhihar, then realized his mistake. He's too weak to fast even two hours without his medication, let alone sixty consecutive days.

For such cases: فَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعَامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِينًا (And he who is unable—then the feeding of sixty poor persons). The verb يَسْتَطِعْ means "is unable," indicating genuine incapacity, not mere inconvenience.

The expiation is feeding sixty مِسْكِين —poor people. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't specify that all sixty must be fed at once in a single gathering. This allows flexibility—you could feed them over time, but until you complete feeding all sixty, the obligation remains unpaid.

Other ayahs in the Quran clarify what this feeding entails: مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ (from the average of that which you feed your families). The food must be equivalent to what you eat yourself—a proper meal, not token handouts. You cannot fulfill this by handing out candy bars to sixty people. These must be genuine meals provided to genuinely poor people, not your friends whom you take out to lunch and count toward your obligation.

The ayah then reveals the deeper purpose: ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ (That is for you to believe in Allah and His Messenger). Why does paying this expiation strengthen iman? Because throughout the sixty days of fasting, or as you feed each of the sixty poor people, or as you work to free that slave, you're constantly reminded of why you're doing this. Every day of that fast, you think: "I'm making up for the wrong I did. I'm paying this penalty so I don't have to answer for this sin in the Akhirah." That constant consciousness, returning day after day, reinforces your belief in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, in accountability, in divine justice, in the reality of the Hereafter.

The ayah concludes: وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ (And those are the limits of Allah). These are boundaries He has set, laws He has established. Then comes an unexpected shift: وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (And for the disbelievers is a painful punishment).

Wait—this entire passage addresses believers. The context is Muslim husbands and wives. Why suddenly mention disbelievers here? Because this is about disbelief in this specific instruction. Someone might think: "How will the Prophet ﷺ know what I did in my house with my wife? This is my private business. I don't need to pay this expiation. I'll just ignore it."

That attitude is a form of kufr—rejection of a clear divine command. The word كَـٰفِرِينَ  here doesn't necessarily mean someone who has left Islam entirely. Rather, it refers to someone in denial of this particular obligation, someone ungrateful for the opportunity to make amends, someone who rejects this boundary Allah ﷻ has set.

translation: Indeed, those who oppose Allah and His Messenger are abased as those before them were abased. And We have certainly sent down verses of clear evidence. And for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآدُّونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ كُبِتُوا۟ كَمَا كُبِتَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ وَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

**Indeed, those who oppose Allah and His Messenger are abased as those before them were abased. And We have certainly sent down clear verses. And for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment.**

This ayah escalates the warning. The verb يُحَآدُّونَ  comes from the root ح-د-د, related to حُدُود —boundaries. Those who يُحَآدُّونَ are those who cross boundaries, who resist, who oppose with the strongest form of opposition. They don't just disagree—they actively work against Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

The consequence: كُبِتُوا۟ (they are abased, humiliated, defeated). This happened to those who opposed divine guidance before, and it will happen again to anyone who takes that path. The ayah reminds us: وَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ (And We have certainly sent down clear verses). The word بَيِّنَـٰتٍ means clear, obvious, unambiguous. These aren't mysterious or difficult teachings. The guidance is crystal clear. There's no excuse for opposition.

Finally: وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (And for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment). Notice the progression from the previous ayah. There it was عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (painful punishment), here it's عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (humiliating punishment). The pain is one thing, but the humiliation—the disgrace of having opposed clear truth, of having rejected manifest guidance—that is another level of consequence entirely.

These opening ayahs of Surah Al-Mujadila teach us several profound lessons. First, Allah ﷻ hears the complaints of the oppressed, especially women suffering injustice in their homes. Second, words matter—we cannot use harmful language and think there are no consequences. Third, family relationships are sacred covenants that cannot be severed through emotional outbursts. Fourth, Islamic law provides both justice and mercy: it condemns the wrong while offering pathways to repentance and reconciliation. Finally, sincere observance of divine boundaries strengthens our iman, while rejection of clear guidance leads to humiliation in this life and the next.

translation: On the Day when Allah will resurrect them all and inform them of what they did. Allah had enumerated it, while they forgot it; and Allah is, over all things, Witness
commentary:
**يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ ۚ أَحْصَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ وَنَسُوهُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ**

*On the Day when Allah will resurrect them all together, then inform them of what they did. Allah has recorded it while they have forgotten it, and Allah is Witness over all things.*

The humiliating punishment will manifest يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا —on the day when Allah ﷻ will gather and raise them all together. Then comes something remarkable: فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ —He will thoroughly inform them of all the things they used to do.

The verb نَبَّأَ  in its simple form means to inform someone of something. But here it appears in the intensified form يُنَبِّئُ , which indicates extensive, thorough information. This won't be a brief reminder—it will be a comprehensive, detailed account. Every action, every word, every hidden deed will be laid bare in full detail.

Then Allah ﷻ reveals the stark contrast between His knowledge and theirs: أَحْصَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ وَنَسُوهُ—Allah has fully recorded it while they have forgotten it. The word أَحْصَىٰ  literally means to count using pebbles. When Arabs wanted an exact count, they would place a pebble for each item—creating an indisputable, complete record. This is Allah's record: perfect, complete, preserved.

Meanwhile, they forgot. This is natural for human beings. We forget what we said, what we did, especially the things we'd rather not remember. Subconsciously, our minds want to block out our mistakes. Sometimes children genuinely forget the trouble they caused because their minds pushed it away. But Allah ﷻ doesn't forget. His record remains intact.

The ayah closes with a powerful statement: وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ —Allah is a constant witness over all things. The word شَهِيدٌ  emphasizes continuous witnessing. He's not just aware; He's constantly present, constantly observing everything that happens.

translation: Have you not considered that Allah knows what is in the heavens and what is on the earth? There is in no private conversation three but that He is the fourth of them, nor are there five but that He is the sixth of them - and no less than that and no more except that He is with them [in knowledge] wherever they are. Then He will inform them of what they did, on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah is, of all things, Knowing
commentary:
**أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ مَا يَكُونُ مِن نَّجْوَىٰ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ رَابِعُهُمْ وَلَا خَمْسَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ سَادِسُهُمْ وَلَآ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْثَرَ إِلَّا هُوَ مَعَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ ۖ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ**

*Have you not seen that Allah knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth? There is no secret counsel of three but He is their fourth, nor of five but He is their sixth, nor less than that or more but He is with them wherever they may be. Then He will inform them of what they did on the Day of Resurrection. Certainly, Allah has full knowledge of all things.*

From one form of corruption behind closed doors, we move to another: نَّجْوَىٰ —secret counsel. To understand this concept, we need to know that Islam recognizes two opposite types of group activity: شُورَىٰ  and نَّجْوَىٰ .

Shura refers to open consultation. Picture the Prophet ﷺ holding a meeting with twenty Companions (RA). He's taking suggestions, everyone voices their opinion openly. Someone proposes an idea, another offers constructive criticism, a third adds support with modifications. There's a back-and-forth exchange leading to a better conclusion. This is productive discussion—open, honest, with everyone present participating toward a shared goal.

Najwa, however, is when a select group within that gathering—say five out of those twenty—hold a meeting after the meeting. They hang out in the parking lot and express opinions they failed to raise during the actual session. They might have genuine concerns, but they're raising them in the wrong venue, with the wrong people, at the wrong time.

Why is this problematic? First, it violates the trust of the gathering. Part of meeting etiquette is that matters discussed there should be addressed openly with everyone present. When you pull aside for a separate discussion, you break that trust.

Second, these smaller groups form what we might call lobbies or voting blocks. When three to five people discuss matters privately first, they develop a unified position. They become more firm and convicted in their view. Now they're not entering the larger meeting with an open mind ready to consider all perspectives—they're coming with a pre-formed agenda. Five people aligned on one opinion have more momentum than fifteen individuals with varying thoughts. They can hijack the discussion.

Third, even when it starts innocently, najwa breeds division and animosity. Meeting over, someone pulls a few friends aside: "Every week this guy says the same thing. I've given him suggestions—he just doesn't get it. Why is he so closed-minded?" The others, perhaps newer Muslims who don't recognize the harm, start agreeing. Division takes root.

This was happening in the time of the Prophet ﷺ. Sometimes hardcore hypocrites would pull aside newer Companions (RA) who didn't understand the danger and would influence them through these private discussions. The more sinister variation was when hypocrites like Abdullah ibn Ubayy—who had been on the verge of becoming Medina's king before Islam arrived—would gather his circle to plot how to disrupt the Prophet's ﷺ work. Since they couldn't openly oppose him, they worked through secret councils to undermine his leadership.

Allah ﷻ begins by establishing His complete knowledge: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ —haven't you realized that Allah knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth? Secret meetings are held precisely because people don't want others to know. But nothing is hidden from Allah ﷻ.

Then comes the striking imagery: مَا يَكُونُ مِن نَّجْوَىٰ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ رَابِعُهُمْ—there is never a secret counsel of three except that He is the fourth with them. وَلَا خَمْسَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ سَادِسُهُمْ —nor five except that He is their sixth.

Notice the deliberate mention of specific numbers. Why three and five? Because these are typical sizes for secret meetings—small enough to feel private, large enough to form a significant faction. But whether it's three people or five, Allah ﷻ is present with complete knowledge.

The ayah continues: وَلَآ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْثَرَ إِلَّا هُوَ مَعَهُمْ —nor any smaller number than that, nor any larger, except that He is with them. Whether it's two people or two hundred, His knowledge encompasses them completely.

أَيْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ —wherever they may be. They don't just pick secret times—late night hours when others are asleep—they pick secret locations where they won't be discovered. Hidden corners, private rooms, isolated spots. None of it matters. Location doesn't hide anything from Allah ﷻ.

Then the reminder repeats: ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ —then He will inform them thoroughly of what they did on the Day of Resurrection. Every whispered word, every hidden plot, every undermining conversation will be replayed in full.

The ayah closes with certainty: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ —certainly Allah has full knowledge of all things. The word عَلِيمٌ  is the intensive form, emphasizing complete, comprehensive, absolute knowledge. Nothing escapes Him.

translation: Have you not considered those who were forbidden from private conversation, then they return to that which they were forbidden and converse among themselves about sin and aggression and disobedience to the Messenger? And when they come to you, they greet you with that [word] by which Allah does not greet you and say among themselves, "Why does Allah not punish us for what we say?" Sufficient for them is Hell, which they will [enter to] burn, and wretched is the destination
commentary:
**أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نُهُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَيَتَنَـٰجَوْنَ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَإِذَا جَآءُوكَ حَيَّوْكَ بِمَا لَمْ يُحَيِّكَ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ وَيَقُولُونَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَوْلَا يُعَذِّبُنَا ٱللَّهُ بِمَا نَقُولُ ۚ حَسْبُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ يَصْلَوْنَهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ**

*Have you not seen those who were forbidden from secret counsel, yet they return to what they were forbidden from? They secretly counsel one another in sin, aggression, and disobedience to the Messenger. And when they come to you, they greet you with what Allah has not greeted you with, and they say within themselves, "Why does Allah not punish us for what we say?" Sufficient for them is Hellfire—they will burn therein. What a miserable destination!*

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نُهُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ —didn't you look to those who were forbidden from this kind of secret counsel? This references an earlier revelation in Surah Yusuf where Allah ﷻ declared: "There is no good in most of their secret counsels" (12:114). They had already received a clear warning.

ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ —then they go back to whatever they were forbidden from. Despite the clear prohibition, they returned to the practice. This shows the depth of their defiance—they heard, they knew, and they deliberately chose to continue.

وَيَتَنَـٰجَوْنَ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ —and they counsel one another secretly in matters of sin, aggression, and disobedience to the Messenger.

Notice three distinct elements here. إِثْمِ  refers to spiritual harm—sins that damage one's relationship with Allah ﷻ, that corrupt faith from within. These secret councils were leading people away from truth, creating obstacles to Allah's work, and therefore harming their iman.

عُدْوَٰنِ  means aggression or animosity—social harm. When cliques form and lobbies develop, they naturally become defensive toward outsiders and aggressive in promoting their agenda. Division and hostility grow. What started as a small group discussion becomes a faction that opposes the larger community.

مَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ —disobedience to the Messenger. Here the Prophet ﷺ isn't mentioned merely as a messenger delivering revelation; he's mentioned as the leader, the head of state, the authority figure these secret councils were working to undermine. After the Prophet ﷺ, this applies to whoever holds legitimate leadership. Secret councils typically form among those opposed to leadership, gathering to disrupt and sabotage rather than offer constructive input.

Then Allah ﷻ exposes their hypocrisy: وَإِذَا جَآءُوكَ حَيَّوْكَ بِمَا لَمْ يُحَيِّكَ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ —and when they come to you, they greet you with what Allah has not greeted you with.

The scholars offer two interpretations. Some say they would literally change the greeting. Instead of saying "As-salamu 'alaykum ya Rasool Allah" (peace be upon you, O Messenger of Allah), they would subtly say "As-samu 'alaykum" (death be upon you), twisting the Arabic to make it sound similar but carry the opposite meaning.

Others understand this more broadly: Allah ﷻ greets His Messenger ﷺ with honor and reverence—إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتَهُۥ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ —"Certainly Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet" (33:56). Yet these hypocrites greeted him without sincerity, with empty words or even subtle mockery. The words might be "salam," but the tone is condescending, the heart is hostile.

Allah ﷻ mentions Himself here specifically because their failure to properly honor His Messenger ﷺ is an affront to Him. If Allah Himself honors His Messenger ﷺ with such high regard, how dare anyone show anything less?

Then Allah ﷻ captures their inner thoughts: وَيَقُولُونَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَوْلَا يُعَذِّبُنَا ٱللَّهُ بِمَا نَقُولُ —and they say within themselves, "Why doesn't Allah punish us for what we say?"

This reveals their twisted logic. They insulted the Prophet ﷺ or spoke against him and nothing immediately happened—no lightning strike, no instant death. So in their arrogance, they concluded: "See? If he really was Allah's Messenger, we would have been destroyed on the spot. Since we're fine, he must be fake."

This thought alone—this internal mockery and doubt—seals their disbelief. They've lost whatever remnant of faith they might have claimed.

Allah's response is swift and final: حَسْبُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ يَصْلَوْنَهَا —sufficient for them is Hellfire; they will burn therein. Allah ﷻ doesn't need to punish them immediately in this world. The punishment that awaits them is far more severe. Hellfire itself is enough. The verb يَصْلَوْنَ  means to be thrown into fire, to be cast into it, to burn completely in it.

فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ —what a miserable destination! What a horrible place to return to.

translation: O you who have believed, when you converse privately, do not converse about sin and aggression and disobedience to the Messenger but converse about righteousness and piety. And fear Allah, to whom you will be gathered
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَنَـٰجَيْتُمْ فَلَا تَتَنَـٰجَوْا۟ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَتَنَـٰجَوْا۟ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ**

*O you who believe! When you hold secret counsel, do not counsel one another in sin, aggression, and disobedience to the Messenger, but counsel one another in righteousness and consciousness of Allah. And fear Allah, to Whom you will all be gathered.*

After condemning the hypocrites, Allah ﷻ now addresses the believers directly: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ —O you who believe! This call is filled with both affection and warning. You claim faith—now prove it through your actions.

إِذَا تَنَـٰجَيْتُمْ —when you hold secret counsel. Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't say "if" but "when." He acknowledges the reality that sometimes believers will need to have private discussions. Islam doesn't forbid all private conversations. There are legitimate reasons for confidentiality: sensitive personal matters, planning a surprise gift, discussing someone's private struggles to help them, strategic planning that requires discretion.

The issue isn't privacy itself—it's the content and intent of that privacy.

فَلَا تَتَنَـٰجَوْا۟ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ —do not counsel one another in sin, aggression, and disobedience to the Messenger. The same three categories mentioned for the hypocrites are now used as boundaries for believers. Don't let your private conversations lead you toward spiritual harm , don't let them breed social division and hostility, and don't let them result in undermining legitimate leadership .

If you find yourself in a meeting after the meeting where people are complaining, criticizing without constructive purpose, forming factions, or planning to oppose rather than support—you're crossing into forbidden territory. Even if your concerns are legitimate, the venue matters. Raise them openly, appropriately, and with the right people present.

وَتَنَـٰجَوْا۟ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ—but counsel one another in righteousness and consciousness of Allah. Here's the alternative, the positive command. If you're going to have private discussions, let them be about birr—goodness, righteousness, acts of kindness and service. Let them be characterized by taqwa—consciousness of Allah, awareness of His presence, concern for His pleasure.

Imagine the difference: instead of a group gathering to complain about leadership, they gather to plan how to support it. Instead of meeting to gossip about someone's faults, they meet to discuss how to help that person. Instead of forming a faction to push their agenda, they come together to sincerely advise and then submit to the collective good. That's najwa characterized by birr and taqwa.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ —and fear Allah, to Whom you will all be gathered. The ayah ends with this powerful reminder. Whatever you discuss in secret, whatever you plan behind closed doors, whatever you think no one else knows—you will be gathered back to Allah ﷻ. He heard it all. He recorded it all. You will answer for it all.

This isn't meant to paralyze us with fear but to elevate our consciousness. Let every conversation, public or private, be conducted with the awareness that Allah ﷻ is present. Let that awareness purify our intentions, guard our tongues, and unite our hearts.

translation: Private conversation is only from Satan that he may grieve those who have believed, but he will not harm them at all except by permission of Allah. And upon Allah let the believers rely
commentary:
إِنَّمَا ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ لِيَحْزُنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَيْسَ بِضَآرِّهِمْ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ

Allah ﷻ reveals the true source and purpose of harmful secret meetings: إِنَّمَا ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ - secret counsel, for the most part, is from Shaytan. The objective behind such gatherings is لِيَحْزُنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - to grieve those who believe.

Picture the scene: believers leave the masjid and notice a group huddled in the parking lot, pointing and whispering. The sense that trouble is brewing becomes palpable. This creates anxiety and grief in the hearts of sincere believers who can see conflict approaching on the horizon.

Yet Allah ﷻ immediately provides reassurance: وَلَيْسَ بِضَآرِّهِمْ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ - it will not harm them in the least except by Allah's permission. The troublemakers' schemes hold no inherent power. Whatever harm may come can only occur by divine decree, and Allah ﷻ never decrees except what contains wisdom.

The ayah concludes with the proper response: وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ - upon Allah alone should the believers place their complete trust. When we witness concerning behavior or sense discord brewing, our refuge lies in tawakkul, not in anxiety or counter-scheming.

translation: O you who have believed, when you are told, "Space yourselves" in assemblies, then make space; Allah will make space for you. And when you are told, "Arise," then arise; Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees. And Allah is Acquainted with what you do
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْمَجَـٰلِسِ فَٱفْسَحُوا۟ يَفْسَحِ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِذَا قِيلَ ٱنشُزُوا۟ فَٱنشُزُوا۟ يَرْفَعِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ دَرَجَـٰتٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

Allah ﷻ provides a practical solution to prevent disruption in gatherings: إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْمَجَـٰلِسِ فَٱفْسَحُوا۟ - when you are told to spread out in the gatherings, then spread out.

Understanding the context illuminates the wisdom here. The Companions (RA) naturally wanted to sit as close as possible to the Prophet ﷺ during his teaching sessions. Everyone desired the front seats, creating clusters of people pressed together. This seating arrangement, while motivated by love and eagerness, created an opportunity for troublemakers.

Hypocrites and those with divisive intentions would arrive late and sit together in the back, forming their own subgroup where they could whisper, make disruptive comments and undermine the proceedings. When the Prophet ﷺ spoke, you might hear scoffing sounds from the back, though you couldn't identify exactly who made them. This protected the troublemakers while disturbing everyone else.

The divine solution was brilliantly simple: spread out like a checkerboard, leaving space between people. Notice the command doesn't say تَفَسَّحُوا - a form that might imply taking your time. Instead, فَٱفْسَحُوا۟ carries the emphatic "fa" that means do it immediately, without delay.

The strategic genius becomes clear: when latecomers arrive, especially those five troublemakers who usually sit together, they now find no space to form their disruptive cluster. The only available seats are scattered between sincere, committed believers. Suddenly the person who came to make trouble finds himself sitting between companions like Umar (RA) and Ali (RA). Will he dare to scoff or whisper now? The physical arrangement has neutralized his ability to cause disruption.

Allah ﷻ connects this obedience to divine expansion: يَفْسَحِ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ - Allah will make space for you. This operates on multiple levels. Literally, space opens in the gathering. Spiritually, Allah ﷻ expands your heart and circumstances. Practically, it hints at territorial expansion and victory for the Muslim community.

The second command addresses what happens when the gathering concludes: وَإِذَا قِيلَ ٱنشُزُوا۟ فَٱنشُزُوا۟ - when you are told to rise, then rise. The word ٱنشُزُوا۟ doesn't mean a casual, leisurely departure. It means to get up abruptly and leave immediately.

This might seem harsh until we understand its preventative wisdom. Typically, when a meeting ends, people begin socializing: "MashaAllah, that was beneficial... How's your family?... Did you hear about..." If people linger and chat, small groups inevitably form. Someone suggests going out for food or coffee. During that social time, what happens? The meeting's contents become the topic of discussion, and that discussion can easily shift into the very نَجْوَىٰ that Allah ﷻ warned against.

The solution: socialize at other times. When a gathering held for Allah's sake concludes, disperse immediately. This isn't about being unfriendly; it's about protecting the sanctity of what just occurred and preventing the backsliding into harmful speech that so easily follows.

Allah ﷻ then reveals the spiritual reward for this discipline: يَرْفَعِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ دَرَجَـٰتٍ - Allah will raise those who believe among you and those who have been given knowledge in ranks.

This creates beautiful spiritual mathematics. These are simple acts - spreading out when asked, leaving promptly when told. Yet observing this etiquette requires genuine discipline, especially when you haven't seen dear friends in a while and naturally want to catch up. Restraining yourself in such moments demonstrates real iman, and Allah ﷻ elevates your spiritual rank for it.

The ayah closes with وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ - Allah is fully aware of what you do. He sees the internal struggle to comply, the desire to socialize balanced against obedience to His command. Every small act of compliance is recorded and rewarded.

translation: O you who have believed, when you [wish to] privately consult the Messenger, present before your consultation a charity. That is better for you and purer. But if you find not [the means] - then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نَـٰجَيْتُمُ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَةً ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ introduces a temporary measure to address another manifestation of hypocrisy. Certain hypocrites had enjoyed political and social influence before Islam, and with the Prophet's ﷺ arrival, they felt their status completely deflated. They devised a strategy to maintain their perceived importance: securing private meetings with the Prophet ﷺ where other Companions could witness them.

The calculation was shrewd. If people saw someone having a private audience with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, they would naturally assume this person held significance. The Prophet ﷺ must consider him important to grant such exclusive time. Thus these individuals would request private meetings - not out of genuine need, but to be seen requesting them and to cultivate an impression of closeness to leadership.

The Prophet ﷺ, with his noble character, would accommodate these requests. But Allah ﷻ revealed a brilliant filter to test the sincerity behind such requests: فَقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَةً - put forward charity before your private consultation.

This was a private meeting "tax" - give sadaqah before meeting with the Prophet ﷺ. Allah ﷻ explains the wisdom: ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ - that is better for you and purer. The charity purifies the intention. Someone seeking a meeting for show would find their true motivations exposed, because the one thing hypocrites typically hate doing is giving charity.

The wealthy hypocrites now faced an uncomfortable choice. They could give the charity, which they despised doing. Or they could invoke the exemption clause: فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - if you don't find the means, then indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. But claiming they couldn't find the wealth meant appearing either cheap or poor - and for people obsessed with maintaining appearances, this was intolerable.

The result? These image-conscious hypocrites stopped requesting meetings altogether. The only person who actually gave charity and proceeded with a private meeting was Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA), the Prophet's son-in-law. As soon as the opportunity presented itself, he gave sadaqah and met with the Messenger ﷺ. The content of that meeting remains private, as it should - that's what the charity paid for.

translation: Have you feared to present before your consultation charities? Then when you do not and Allah has forgiven you, then [at least] establish prayer and give zakah and obey Allah and His Messenger. And Allah is Acquainted with what you do
commentary:
ءَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَن تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَـٰتٍ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَتَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ addresses the community with a penetrating question: ءَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَن تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَـٰتٍ - Were you afraid to present charity before your private consultation? The verb أَشْفَقْتُمْ carries connotations of fear, apprehension and holding back.

This divine question exposes the reality: the requirement revealed who truly needed to meet with the Prophet ﷺ versus who simply wanted to be seen meeting with him. Those with genuine concerns would gladly give charity for the opportunity. Those seeking status found the price too high.

Allah ﷻ then lifts this particular requirement: فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَتَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ - Since you did not do it and Allah has turned to you in acceptance. This wasn't a permanent obligation but a temporary test that served its purpose. Having filtered out the insincere, Allah ﷻ in His mercy removes the requirement.

But the ayah doesn't end there. Having addressed this specific situation, Allah ﷻ pivots to universal, permanent obligations: فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ - So establish prayer, give zakah, and obey Allah and His Messenger.

The structure here teaches a profound lesson. The charity before private meetings was temporary. But don't think you've escaped financial responsibility - zakah remains permanent. The requirement for sincerity in approaching religious leadership was specific to that context. But don't assume you can now be casual about obedience - perfect compliance to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ remains mandatory.

وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - Allah is fully aware of what you do. This concluding reminder appears throughout these ayahs, consistently redirecting our attention to the reality that no act goes unnoticed. Whether we comply with temporary measures or permanent obligations, whether our intentions are pure or polluted, Allah ﷻ knows completely.

translation: Have you not considered those who make allies of a people with whom Allah has become angry? They are neither of you nor of them, and they swear to untruth while they know [they are lying]
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مَّا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَا مِنْهُمْ وَيَحْلِفُونَ عَلَى ٱلْكَذِبِ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

The discourse shifts to identify a particularly dangerous category of people: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم - Have you not looked toward those who have turned in loyalty to a people with whom Allah is angry?

While this connects immediately to those who avoided meeting the Prophet ﷺ with the wrong intentions, Allah ﷻ is describing something deeper: the character of hypocrites who have turned away from the Muslim mission entirely. Though they declined private meetings with the Messenger ﷺ, their turning away represents a broader abandonment of sincere commitment to Islam.

The description that follows captures their liminal state: مَّا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَا مِنْهُمْ - They are neither from you nor from them. Having declared Islam outwardly, the disbelievers no longer accept them as fellow disbelievers. Yet their hearts never truly embraced faith, so they aren't genuinely among the believers either. They exist in a wretched middle space, belonging nowhere, accepted by no one.

This spiritual homelessness marks the tragedy of hypocrisy. The hypocrite gains nothing - not the brotherhood of believers, not even the solidarity of those who openly reject faith. They remain perpetually alienated, trusted by none.

وَيَحْلِفُونَ عَلَى ٱلْكَذِبِ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ - They swear to lies while they know they are lying. Their oaths become tools of deception. What do they swear? "I bear witness that there is no deity except Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah." They proclaim this with their tongues, swearing by Allah Himself, yet they know in their hearts they don't believe it. This represents the ultimate abuse of oaths - weaponizing sacred testimony in service of falsehood.

translation: Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment. Indeed, it was evil that they were doing
commentary:
أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares the consequence awaiting such people: أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا - Allah has prepared for them severe punishment. The word أَعَدَّ indicates preparation that has already occurred. Their punishment isn't merely planned or potential; it stands ready, waiting for them.

The severity stems from their unique treachery. Open enemies are straightforward to identify and defend against. But hypocrites undermine from within, wearing the garments of friendship while harboring hostile intentions. They corrupt the community's trust, sow discord in private meetings and weaponize sacred oaths for worldly gain.

إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - How terrible are the deeds they used to do. The expression سَآءَ indicates something at the extreme end of wickedness. Their actions weren't merely wrong; they represented some of the worst forms of behavior - betraying trust, lying under oath about ultimate truth, and working against divine guidance while claiming to support it.

translation: They took their [false] oaths as a cover, so they averted [people] from the way of Allah, and for them is a humiliating punishment
commentary:
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

The hypocrites discovered a clever way to weaponize their faith: they turned their shahada into a shield. Consider what the shahada represents. One of its beautiful benefits is protection. When the Prophet ﷺ taught us about the true Muslim, he described them as someone from whose hands and tongue other Muslims are safe. This creates a sanctuary of trust within the Muslim community.

But the hypocrites twisted this sacred trust. Allah ﷻ tells us: اتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً - they took their oaths as a shield. The word جُنَّة  means a protective barrier, a shield that deflects harm. By outwardly professing Islam, they created cover for themselves. They could engage in whatever covert activities they wanted to undermine the Muslim community, and whenever questioned or confronted, they simply had to say, "I'm Muslim. You can't touch me. I'm one of you."

This shield gave them freedom to operate. فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ  - then they obstructed from the path of Allah ﷻ. They blocked themselves and others from His path. Their treacherous activities were being conducted under the shade of Islam itself, making them even more dangerous than open enemies.

فَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ  - for them is a humiliating punishment. Notice the specific type of punishment: not just painful, but humiliating. They sought to hide behind respectability while scheming in the shadows. Their punishment will match their crime - complete humiliation before Allah ﷻ.

translation: Never will their wealth or their children avail them against Allah at all. Those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
لَّن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

Their wealth and their children - these worldly sources of power and legacy - will not benefit them against Allah ﷻ in any way, shape or form. لَّن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ  - never will they avail them of anything.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ  - those are the companions of the Fire. The word أَصْحَـٰبُ  implies permanent companionship. هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ  - they will remain in it eternally.

translation: On the Day Allah will resurrect them all, and they will swear to Him as they swear to you and think that they are [standing] on something. Unquestionably, it is they who are the liars
commentary:
يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُۥ كَمَا يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ

On the Day Allah ﷻ gathers them all together, something remarkable will happen. These hypocrites became such professionals at their lies, so skilled at looking sincere when taking their oaths, that they'll actually attempt the same performance before Allah ﷻ Himself.

فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُۥ كَمَا يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ  - they will swear to Him just as they swear to you. They've rehearsed their convincing lies of loyalty so many times that on Judgment Day, they'll instinctively start their award-winning performance again, making their pleading case just as they did with the believers in this world.

وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ - and they assume that they are upon something. The figurative meaning is powerful: they think they stand on some ground, that they have some basis, that they've accomplished something with their deception. Even before Allah ﷻ, they believe their performance will work.

أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ - No indeed! They are the liars. The emphatic structure leaves no doubt about their reality.

translation: Satan has overcome them and made them forget the remembrance of Allah. Those are the party of Satan. Unquestionably, the party of Satan - they will be the losers
commentary:
ٱسْتَحْوَذَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ ذِكْرَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حِزْبُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

The word اسْتَحْوَذَ  reveals the depth of Shaytan's control. The root حوذ relates to when you become so skilled at driving a camel that you can steer it whichever way you want with just the slightest indication. You've developed such an organic relationship with the animal that it responds to your every cue. You have total and absolute control over the creature.

Some people develop this mastery with their vehicles - they can maneuver with precision because they have complete control. This is the image Allah ﷻ presents: Shaytan has gained complete control over these individuals. He doesn't have to come and do extensive whispering anymore. They're on autopilot. Shaytan just gives the slightest signal and they act. They are whipped. They are under his complete command, ready at his slightest indication.

فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ ذِكْرَ ٱللَّهِ  - then he made them forget the remembrance of Allah ﷻ. This is one of the heaviest subjects in the Quran at a philosophical level. When you're completely under Shaytan's influence, you don't just disobey Allah ﷻ - you forget Him entirely. The remembrance of Allah ﷻ disappears from your consciousness.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حِزْبُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ  - those are the party of Shaytan. Notice the contrast being set up. The hypocrites follow Shaytan's whims so completely that they don't even require active temptation anymore. They've become his party, his group, his loyal followers.

أَلَآ إِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ - No indeed! The party of Shaytan - they are the ultimate losers.

translation: Indeed, the ones who oppose Allah and His Messenger - those will be among the most humbled
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآدُّونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْأَذَلِّينَ

Notice how this ayah mirrors the beginning of the surah. The surah opened with those who oppose Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, and now it returns to this theme at the conclusion. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآدُّونَ  - those who sharply oppose, who continue to oppose in the harshest way.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْأَذَلِّينَ  - those are among the most humiliated. Not just humiliated, but الْأَذَلِّينَ  - in the midst of those who are the most thoroughly humiliated.

translation: Allah has written, "I will surely overcome, I and My messengers." Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might
commentary:
كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا۠ وَرُسُلِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ

كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ  - Allah ﷻ has ordained, declared, written: لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا۠ وَرُسُلِىٓ  - I will absolutely dominate, I and My messengers. This is a divine decree, an unbreakable promise.

From this ayah, some scholars have derived that Allah ﷻ does not allow His messengers to be overcome by their enemies. If they die, they die after the mission is accomplished, or if they pass, the opposing nation is destroyed. They are not destroyed while their mission remains incomplete. The messengers will dominate in this world.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ - Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Mighty and possessing ultimate authority. He has the power to fulfill this promise, and the authority to decree it.

translation: You will not find a people who believe in Allah and the Last Day having affection for those who oppose Allah and His Messenger, even if they were their fathers or their sons or their brothers or their kindred. Those - He has decreed within their hearts faith and supported them with spirit from Him. And We will admit them to gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally. Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him - those are the party of Allah. Unquestionably, the party of Allah - they are the successful
commentary:
لَّا تَجِدُ قَوْمًا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ يُوَآدُّونَ مَنْ حَآدَّ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ أَوْ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ أَوْ إِخْوَٰنَهُمْ أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَتَبَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ وَأَيَّدَهُم بِرُوحٍ مِّنْهُ ۖ وَيُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حِزْبُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

لَّا تَجِدُ قَوْمًا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ يُوَآدُّونَ مَنْ حَآدَّ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ  - You will not find a people who truly believe in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day showing mutual love to those who oppose Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

This was a test of faith that most of us will never face. We must understand the context carefully. In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, the Sahaba (RA) weren't just dealing with non-Muslims. They were dealing with enemies of Islam - there's a critical difference. These weren't simply people who didn't accept the message. These were individuals who were bloodthirsty for the Prophet ﷺ, who wanted nothing more than for the Quran to disappear and Islam to be wiped out. They were actively working to murder Muslims and destroy the community.

Some of these enemies were family members of the Sahaba (RA). This is not comparable to having non-Muslim family today. Your non-Muslim family members may not understand or like Islam, and that's possible, but they're not bloodthirsty for the Prophet ﷺ. They're not enemies of the faith attempting to annihilate it. They're not murderers of Muslims. The situation the Sahaba (RA) faced was entirely different.

Allah ﷻ says it's not possible for someone to hold true iman in their heart for Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ while simultaneously sharing love with anyone who actively opposes them. The opposition described here refers to the mushrikun of Makkah and the treacherous elements among some of the Jewish tribes who were engaged in active warfare and conspiracy against the Muslims.

وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ  - even if they were their fathers. أَوْ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ  - or their children. أَوْ إِخْوَٰنَهُمْ - or their brothers. أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ  - or their extended families.

Think about what Allah ﷻ is asking. Islam teaches us to be the best to our parents, even if they're non-Muslims. It teaches us to give the rights to our children. These are universal principles. But in this particular case - when your parents or children happen to be direct enemies of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, when they're part of the enemy forces actively opposing Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ - then even that universal principle of maintaining family ties is superseded.

This is why this ayah cannot be understood outside its specific historical context. It's particular to the legacy of our Messenger ﷺ and the unique challenge faced by the Sahaba (RA). It's very dangerous to take this ayah and make a universal rule out of it for any situation. These were very particular cases that, for the most part, don't exist anymore in the way they did then.

The Sahaba (RA) were put in a position that most of us will probably never face. Their iman was tested through who they could love in their own families. SubhanAllah, it's such a difficult test - the hardest thing to do is to change who you love and who you hate. When you love someone, you can't just stop loving them. When you hate someone, you can't just stop hating them. Yet these believers were called to do exactly that.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَتَبَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ - Those are the people in whose hearts Allah ﷻ has written iman. The word كَتَبَ suggests something permanently engraved, like a tattoo etched into their hearts. That is what you call true faith - when you're able to channel the direction of your love for the sake of your love for Allah ﷻ, His Messenger ﷺ and His Book.

وَأَيَّدَهُم بِرُوحٍ مِّنْهُ - and He aided them with a spirit from Him. Some scholars relate this to comfort and tranquility, others to strength from Allah ﷻ. They had to cross such a difficult emotional barrier - severing ties with beloved family members who were enemies of Islam - and Allah ﷻ gave them special support to bear this burden.

وَيُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا  - and He will enter them into gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally.

رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ  - Allah ﷻ is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him. Despite all the sadness surrounding them in this world - the severed family ties, the emotional pain - they found happiness with Allah ﷻ. They were content with His decree.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حِزْبُ ٱللَّهِ - Those are the party of Allah ﷻ. This noble term has unfortunately been hijacked by certain political groups today, but in the Quran it carries profound meaning. It's used for people who were ready to sever all ties of love when Allah ﷻ asked them to, because of their love for Him and His Messenger ﷺ.

Notice the structure of these final ayahs. Earlier, Allah ﷻ mentioned حِزْبُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ  - the party of Shaytan - those on autopilot, ready to do whatever Shaytan asks. Now He mentions His own party. And the hardest thing to do is to change who you love and who you hate. The few Sahaba (RA) who were able to do this - to redirect their love and loyalty purely for Allah ﷻ - earned this title: they're in Allah's group, they're His party.

أَلَآ إِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ  - No indeed! The party of Allah ﷻ - they are the ones who will attain ultimate success.

The surah concludes by drawing the complete contrast: on one side, the party of Shaytan who have forgotten Allah ﷻ entirely; on the other, the party of Allah ﷻ who loved Him so much they were willing to sever the deepest human bonds when required. One group faces ultimate loss and humiliation; the other achieves eternal success and divine pleasure.
